1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechAcyclic.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/PresheafCech.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FreePresheafComplex.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechBridge.lean
8	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
9	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
10	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
11	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
12	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
13	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
14	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	
18	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
19	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
20	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
22	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
23	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
24	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
25	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
26	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
27	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
28	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
29	
30	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
31	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
32	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
33	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
34	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
35	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
36	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
37	\[
38	  \begin{array}{ccc}
39	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
40	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
41	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
42	  \end{array}
43	\]
44	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
45	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
46	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
47	
48	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
54	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
56	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
57	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
58	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
59	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
60	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
62	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
63	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
64	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
65	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
66	  % the unit ideal, see
67	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
68	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
69	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
70	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
71	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
72	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
73	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
74	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
75	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
76	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
77	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
78	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
79	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
80	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
81	  \[
82	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
83	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
84	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
85	  \]
86	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
87	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
88	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
89	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
90	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
91	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
92	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
93	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
94	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
95	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
96	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
97	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
98	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
99	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
100	\end{definition}
101	
102	\begin{definition}
103	
104	\leanok
105	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
106	  \label{def:cech_complex}
107	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
108	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
109	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
110	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
113	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
114	  % is identified with the complex
115	  % $$
116	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
117	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
118	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
119	  % \ldots
120	  % $$
121	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
122	  % \begin{equation}
123	  % \label{equation-extended}
124	  % 0 \to
125	  % M \to
126	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
127	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
128	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
129	  % \ldots
130	  % \end{equation}
131	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
132	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
133	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
134	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
135	  vanishing).}
136	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
137	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
138	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
139	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
140	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
141	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
142	  in degree \(p\) it is
143	  \[
144	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
145	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
146	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
147	  \]
148	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
149	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
150	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
151	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
152	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
153	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
154	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
155	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
156	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
157	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
158	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
159	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
160	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
161	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
162	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
163	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
164	\end{definition}
165	
166	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
167	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
168	
169	It is convenient to factor the construction of
170	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
171	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
172	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
173	
174	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
175	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
176	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
177	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
178	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
179	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
180	\[
181	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
182	    \;=\;
183	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
184	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
185	\]
186	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
187	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
188	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
189	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
190	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
191	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
192	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
193	
194	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
195	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
196	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
197	\[
198	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
199	  \qquad
200	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
201	\]
202	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
203	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
204	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
205	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
206	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
207	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
208	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
209	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
210	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
211	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
212	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
213	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
214	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
215	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
216	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
217	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
218	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
219	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
220	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
221	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
223	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
224	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
225	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
226	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
227	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
228	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
229	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
230	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
231	
232	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
233	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
234	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
235	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
236	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
237	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
238	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
239	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
240	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
241	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
242	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
243	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
244	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
245	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
246	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
247	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
248	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
249	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
250	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
251	
252	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
253	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
254	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
255	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
256	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
257	
258	\begin{definition}\leanok
259	[Arrow of an affine cover]
260	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
261	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
262	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
263	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
264	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
265	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
266	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
267	  apply.
268	\end{definition}
269	
270	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
271	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
273	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
274	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
275	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
276	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
277	  \[
278	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
279	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
280	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
281	  \]
282	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
283	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
284	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
285	\end{definition}
286	
287	\begin{definition}\leanok
288	[Push--pull object map]
289	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
291	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
292	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
293	  \[
294	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
295	  \]
296	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
297	\end{definition}
298	
299	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
300	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
302	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
303	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
304	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
305	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
306	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
307	  \[
308	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
310	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
312	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \;=\;
314	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
316	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
317	  \]
318	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
319	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
320	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
321	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
322	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
323	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
324	\end{definition}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
328	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
330	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
331	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
332	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
333	\end{lemma}
334	
335	\begin{proof}
336	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
337	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
338	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
339	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
340	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
341	\end{proof}
342	
343	\begin{lemma}
344	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
345	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
347	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
348	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
349	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
350	  \[
351	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
352	  \]
353	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
354	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
355	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
356	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
357	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
358	\end{lemma}
359	
360	\begin{proof}
361	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
362	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
363	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
364	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
365	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
366	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
367	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
368	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
369	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
370	\end{proof}
371	
372	\begin{lemma}
373	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
374	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
376	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
377	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
378	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
379	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
380	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
381	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
382	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
383	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
384	  \[
385	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
386	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
387	    \;=\;
388	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
389	  \]
390	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
391	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
392	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
393	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
394	\end{lemma}
395	
396	\begin{proof}
397	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
398	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
399	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\begin{lemma}
403	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
404	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
406	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
407	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
408	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
409	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
410	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
411	  collapses to the transport-light form
412	  \[
413	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
414	      \;\circ\; e_2
415	    \;=\;
416	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
417	      \;\circ\; e_3,
418	  \]
419	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
420	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
421	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
422	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
423	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
424	  composition (pentagon) law.
425	\end{lemma}
426	
427	\begin{proof}
428	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
429	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
430	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\begin{definition}
434	[Push--pull functor]
435	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
436	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
437	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
438	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
439	  \[
440	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
441	  \]
442	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
443	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
444	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
445	  laws established above: the identity law
446	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
447	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
448	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
449	\end{definition}
450	
451	\begin{definition}
452	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
453	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
455	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
456	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
457	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
458	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
459	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
460	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
461	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
462	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
463	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
464	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
465	\end{definition}
466	
467	\begin{definition}\leanok
468	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
469	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
470	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
471	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
472	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
473	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
474	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
475	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
476	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
477	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
478	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
479	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
480	\end{definition}
481	
482	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
483	
484	\begin{definition}
485	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
486	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
487	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
488	  \mathlibok
489	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
490	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
491	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
492	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
493	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
494	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
495	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
496	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
497	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
498	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
499	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
500	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
501	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
502	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
503	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
504	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
505	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
506	\end{definition}
507	
508	\begin{lemma}
509	
510	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech-complex vanishing on affines]
511	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine,
513	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.combDifferential,
514	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.combHomotopy,

519	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.combSign_flip,

522	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depDiff,
523	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depHomotopy,
524	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depHomotopy_spec,
525	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depTransport,
526	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.cons_comp_zero_succAbove,
527	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.comp_succAbove_swap,
528	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depDiff_eq_of_cocycle,
529	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depDiff_comp,
530	    AlgebraicGeometry.CombinatorialCech.depDiff_exact}
531	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
532	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
533	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
534	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52).
535	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
536	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
537	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
538	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
539	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
540	  % all $p > 0$."
541	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
542	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
543	  vanishing).}
544	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
545	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
546	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
547	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). The cover is presented as the
548	  spanning-family bundle \((s : \iota \to A,\; hs : \operatorname{Ideal.span}
549	  (\operatorname{range} s) = \top)\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}
550	  rather than as a general open cover, so the index set and the affine pieces
551	  \(D(s_i)\) with sections \(A_{s_i}\) are read off directly from the spanning
552	  family. Then the {\v C}ech complex
553	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
554	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} has vanishing cohomology in all positive
555	  degrees:
556	  \[
557	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
558	  \]
559	  Equivalently, the extended complex
560	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}}
561	  \to \cdots\) of localisations is exact. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech
562	  vanishing only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing
563	  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the separate
564	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
565	  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
569	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
570	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
571	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
572	% ...
573	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
574	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
575	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
576	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
577	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
578	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
579	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
580	% Let us define a homotopy
581	% $$
582	% h :
583	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
584	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
585	% \longrightarrow
586	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
587	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
588	% $$
589	% by the rule
590	% $$
591	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
592	% $$
593	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
594	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
595	\begin{proof}
596	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
597	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
598	  \(A\)-module \(M\).
599	
600	  \paragraph{Identification of the abstract {\v C}ech complex with the localisation
601	  complex (the categorical--module bridge).}
602	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
603	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691--719).
604	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
605	  % as an $R_f$-module.
606	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
607	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
608	  % are the maps $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
609	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
610	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
611	  items (4)--(5).}
612	  The {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is built from the
613	  \emph{abstract} cover \(\mathcal{U} = (\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\;
614	  s\; hs).\mathrm{openCover}\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}, whose
615	  degree-\(p\) term is an object of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} A)\); we
616	  record explicitly how those terms and their differential become the concrete
617	  complex of localised modules that the homotopy argument operates on (writing
618	  \(s_i = f_i\) for the spanning family). First, the \(i\)-th piece of
619	  \(\mathcal{U}\) is \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_{s_i}) \to \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the
620	  open immersion of the basic open \(D(s_i)\); the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection
621	  indexed by a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) is the basic open
622	  \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
623	  \[
624	    s_\sigma \;=\; \prod_{k=0}^{p} s_{\sigma(k)}
625	      \;=\; s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)},
626	  \]
627	  because the basic opens of an affine satisfy \(D(s_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
628	  D(s_{i_p}) = D(s_{i_0} \cdots s_{i_p})\); its sections form the \emph{away}
629	  localisation \(A_{s_\sigma} = A_{s_{\sigma(0)} \cdots s_{\sigma(p)}}\). Second,
630	  because \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent, item~(4) of the cited
631	  lemma identifies its sections over each basic open with the away localisation of
632	  the module,
633	  \[
634	    \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr) \;=\; M_{s_\sigma},
635	  \]
636	  the localisation of \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by the product
637	  \(s_\sigma\) (the \(\operatorname{Localization.Away}\) /
638	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) localisation of \(M\) at \(s_\sigma\)). Hence
639	  the degree-\(p\) term of the abstract {\v C}ech complex is, term by term, the
640	  product of away localisations
641	  \[
642	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
643	      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota}
644	              \Gamma\bigl(D(s_\sigma), \mathcal{F}\bigr)
645	      \;=\; \prod_{\sigma : \{0,\ldots,p\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma}.
646	  \]
647	  Third, the {\v C}ech differential of the abstract complex --- the alternating sum
648	  \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1}(-1)^j\) of the restriction maps along the face inclusions
649	  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\) obtained by omitting the
650	  \(j\)-th index (so \(D(s_\sigma) \subset D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})\), the product
651	  \(s_\sigma\) carrying the extra factor \(s_{\sigma(j)}\)) --- is, by item~(5) of
652	  the cited lemma (the restriction of \(\widetilde M\) along an inclusion of basic
653	  opens \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is precisely the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)),
654	  exactly the alternating sum of the localisation maps
655	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) between the away localisations. The
656	  degreewise identification of the second step therefore intertwines the abstract
657	  {\v C}ech coboundary with the concrete localised coboundary: it is an isomorphism
658	  of cochain complexes from \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
659	  onto the concrete complex of away localisations. Consequently the positive-degree
660	  vanishing \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)\)
661	  of the abstract complex is equivalent to positive-degree exactness of the concrete
662	  localised complex, and it is the latter that the local-to-global criterion and the
663	  contracting homotopy below establish.
664	
665	  By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
666	  the standard cover is therefore the complex of localisations
667	  \[
668	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
669	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
670	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
671	  \]
672	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
673	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
674	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}).
675	  Exactness may be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime
676	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\) (Algebra, Lemma
677	  \texttt{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}). Choose an index
678	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
679	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
680	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
681	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
682	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
683	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
684	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
685	  \(p > 0\).
686	
687	  Concretely, exactness is established node-by-node from the spanning family
688	  \((s, hs)\) of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}: the local-to-global
689	  exactness criterion for a span of the unit ideal localises the complex at the
690	  spanning elements (the away localisations \(A_{s_r}\)), reducing positive-degree
691	  exactness of the extended complex to exactness of each localised complex; in the
692	  localisation \(A_{s_r}\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes
693	  \(s_r\) invertible, so the contracting homotopy
694	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{r\, i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is defined globally on that
695	  localisation and contracts it.
696	
697	  \paragraph{The categorical--module bridge (L1), as a formalizable infrastructure
698	  decomposition.}
699	  The combinatorial contracting homotopy above and its consequence
700	  \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) are purely formal once the abstract {\v C}ech
701	  complex has been transported to the concrete cochain complex of localised
702	  \(A\)-modules; the substantive remaining work is exactly that transport, which we
703	  now decompose into three independent pieces, each a self-contained statement a
704	  module-level argument can discharge.
705	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
706	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L692--707).
707	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
708	  % as an $R_f$-module.
709	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
710	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
711	  % are the maps $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
712	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
713	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
714	  items (4)--(5).}
715	
716	  \emph{(1) Section identification.} For the standard cover by basic opens
717	  \(D(s_\sigma)\) with \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), the sections of
718	  \(\operatorname{pushPullObj} \mathcal{F}\) over the basic open
719	  \(D(s_\sigma) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\) are the away localisation
720	  \(M_{s_\sigma}\) of \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\). The
721	  module-level input is Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the
722	  instance \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\)
723	  of the comparison map \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) and
724	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) (that \(g\) acts invertibly on
725	  the sections over \(D(g)\)); quasicoherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) connects
726	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(\widetilde{M}\). By item~(4) of Tag~01HV,
727	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\) as an \(R_g\)-module, and by item~(5) the
728	  restriction along \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\).
729	
730	  \emph{(2) Differential identification.} Under (1), the abstract
731	  \(\operatorname{relativeCechComplexOfNerve}\) / cosimplicial differential becomes the
732	  alternating localisation coboundary, i.e.\ the concrete \(\delta\) feeding
733	  \(\operatorname{depDiff}\) of the dependent combinatorial port. The bridge must
734	  discharge three compatibility hypotheses, which are exactly the interface of that
735	  dependent port: \(\mathtt{hu}\) (unit: \(c \circ \delta_0 = \operatorname{id}\)),
736	  \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (shift: \(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)), and
737	  \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) (coface commutation, that the localisation cofaces commute as
738	  required by the cosimplicial identities). Here \(c\) is the
739	  prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) and the
740	  \(\delta_k\) are the localisation cofaces.
741	
742	  \emph{(3) Homology \(\leftrightarrow\) exactness.} The goal
743	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\bigl((\operatorname{CechComplex} \ldots).\mathrm{homology}\,
744	  p\bigr)\) reduces, via the sectionwise identification on the affine base of (1)--(2),
745	  to \(\operatorname{Function.Exact} d^{p-1}\, d^{p}\) of the underlying \(R\)-module
746	  cochain complex. Each positive-degree node is fed through
747	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\),
748	  localising at the spanning elements (the \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)) so that
749	  exactness is checked one localisation at a time; in the localisation at \(s_r\) the
750	  fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes the prepend homotopy global, and the node
751	  is closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent port. The constant
752	  and dependent combinatorial cores (the \((dh+hd) = \operatorname{id}\) identity in
753	  both the constant-coefficient and the dependent-coefficient settings) and the
754	  spanning-family certifier are already in place; (1)--(3) are the only remaining
755	  infrastructure.
756	
757	  This bridge is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
758	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
759	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
760	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
761	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
762	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
763	  is used instead.
764	\end{proof}

766	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
767	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
768	
769	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
770	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
771	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
772	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
773	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
774	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
775	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
776	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
777	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
778	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
779	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
780	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
781	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
782	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
783	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
784	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
785	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
786	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
787	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
788	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
789	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
790	
791	\begin{definition}
792	
793	\leanok
794	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
795	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
796	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
797	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
798	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
799	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,
800	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen_comp_le,
801	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,
802	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex_X,
803	    AlgebraicGeometry.sigma_ι_eqToHom_transport}
804	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
805	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
806	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
807	  % L1095--1135).
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
809	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
810	  % $$
811	  % \ldots
812	  % \to
813	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
814	  % \to
815	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
816	  % \to
817	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
818	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
819	  % $$
820	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
821	  % and where the map
822	  % $$
823	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
824	  % \longrightarrow
825	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
826	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
827	  % $$
828	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
829	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
830	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
831	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
832	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
833	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
834	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
835	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
836	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
837	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
838	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
839	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
840	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
841	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
842	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
843	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
844	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
845	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
846	  \[
847	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
848	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
849	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
850	  \]
851	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
852	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
853	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
854	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
855	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
856	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
857	  \[
858	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
859	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
860	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
861	  \]
862	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
863	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
864	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
865	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
866	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
867	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
868	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
869	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
870	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
871	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
872	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
873	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
874	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
875	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
876	
877	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
878	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
879	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
880	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
881	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
882	\end{definition}
883	
884	\begin{definition}
885	
886	\leanok
887	[Section {\v C}ech complex of a presheaf of modules]

890	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicial}

900	  % \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0\ldots i_p}).
901	  % $$
902	  % This is an abelian group. ... We define
903	  % $$
904	  % d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
905	  % \longrightarrow

907	  % $$

917	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
918	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
919	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
920	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
921	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
922	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
923	  \[
924	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
925	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
926	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),

928	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
929	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
930	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
931	  \[

933	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,

940	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
941	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair,
273	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ₁, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.f_comp_horseshoeβ₁,
274	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.g_comp_horseshoeH,
275	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero,
276	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero_hf, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_zero,
277	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_fst,
278	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_snd, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0,
279	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_τZero,
280	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero}
281	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
282	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
283	  \[
284	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
285	  \]
286	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
287	  identity
288	  \[
289	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
290	  \]
291	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
292	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
293	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
294	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
295	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
300	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
301	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
302	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
304	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
305	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
306	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
307	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
308	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
309	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
310	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
311	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
313	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
314	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
315	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
316	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
317	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
318	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
319	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
322	\end{proof}

324	\begin{lemma}\leanok
325	[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
326	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
327	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_comp}
328	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
329	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
330	  differential degreewise by the matrix
331	  \[
332	    d_B^n \;=\;
333	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
334	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
335	  \]
336	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
337	  cochain complex.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
342	  \leanok
343	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
344	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
345	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
346	  \[
347	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
348	  \]
349	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
350	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
351	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
352	\end{proof}

354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ,
360	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_X, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_d,
361	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_fst, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_snd,
362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}
363	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
364	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
368	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
369	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
370	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
371	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
372	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.

377	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
378	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
379	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
380	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
381	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
382	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
383	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
384	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
385	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
386	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
387	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.

390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}
