428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
430	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
432	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
434	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561

565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,

573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.

580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is

589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[

593	  \]

606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
700	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

924	\begin{theorem}\leanok
925	[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]

931	  % NOTE (iter-009 review): the Lean encodes the augmentation in the

938	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
939	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
940	  % L5785--5811).
941	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
942	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
943	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
944	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
945	  % $$
946	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
947	  % $$

950	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
951	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
952	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
953	  % and we obtain an exact functor
954	  % $$

956	  % $$

959	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
960	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let
964	  \[
965	    0 \longrightarrow A
966	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
967	      \longrightarrow \cdots
968	  \]
969	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
970	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
971	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
972	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
973	  \[
974	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
975	  \]
976	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
977	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
978	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
979	  object.
980	\end{theorem}
981	
982	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
983	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
984	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
985	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
986	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
987	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
988	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
989	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
990	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
991	% the long exact cohomology sequence
992	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
58

1170	  \]
1171	  defines a contracting homotopy: a direct computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\),
1172	  so the identity of \(K^{\mathrm{ext}}_\bullet(W)\) is homotopic to zero. Hence the
1173	  extended complex is exact over every \(W\), which is the claimed vanishing of the
1174	  homology presheaves.
1175	
1176	  This sectionwise reduction is legitimate because homology of a complex of presheaves
1177	  of modules is computed sectionwise: the evaluation functor
1178	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation}(-)\,V\) at each open \(V\) preserves
1179	  homology (it is exact, so the instance is inferred directly), whence
1180	  \(H_i(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet)(V) = H_i\bigl(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\bigr)\). The
1181	  contracting homotopy \(h\) above is the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map — the same
1182	  combinatorial content as the prepend homotopy of the \texttt{CombinatorialCech.*}
1183	  port appearing in the affine vanishing Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
1184	\end{proof}

1186	\begin{lemma}[Right adjoint of a mono-preserving left adjoint preserves injectives]
1187	  \label{lem:injective_of_adjoint}
1188	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.injective_of_adjoint}
1189	  \mathlibok
1190	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1191	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Preadditive.Injective.Basic}).}
1192	  Let \(L \dashv R\) be an adjunction between abelian (or, more generally, suitable)
1193	  categories such that the left adjoint \(L\) preserves monomorphisms. Then for every
1194	  injective object \(J\) of the target, \(R(J)\) is an injective object of the source.
1195	\end{lemma}

1240	  \texttt{lemma-injective-trivial-cech}.}
1241	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{U} : U =
1242	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be any open covering, and let \(\mathcal{I}\) be an
1243	  \emph{injective} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the {\v C}ech cohomology of
1244	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is concentrated in degree zero:
1245	  \[
1246	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
1247	    \begin{cases}
1248	      \mathcal{I}(U) & p = 0, \\
1249	      0 & p > 0.
1250	    \end{cases}
1251	  \]
1252	\end{lemma}

1254	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "An injective $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is also injective as an
1255	% object in
1256	% the category $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ (for example since
1257	% sheafification is an exact left adjoint to the inclusion functor,
1258	% using Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-adjoint-preserve-injectives}).
1259	% Hence we can apply Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}
1260	% (or its proof) to see the result."
1261	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of
1262	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1263	% In this case the functor $\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})$
1264	% is exact on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. By
1265	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
1266	% $$
1267	% \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
1268	% =
1269	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
1270	% $$
1271	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
1272	% quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
1273	% the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
1274	% that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
1275	% $i > 0$."
1276	\begin{proof}
1277	  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:section_cech_complex, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification, lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso, lem:injective_of_adjoint, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
1278	  The argument has two independent parts.
1279	
1280	  \emph{Part 1: an injective sheaf is injective as a presheaf.} Sheafification is
1281	  left adjoint to the inclusion
1282	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1283	  of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules into presheaves of
1284	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), and its left
1285	  adjoint sheafification is exact, hence preserves monomorphisms. By
1286	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} the right adjoint \(\iota\) carries injective
1287	  objects to injective objects; thus an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
1288	  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective as an object of \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\). In
1289	  particular the contravariant functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-,
1290	  \mathcal{I})\) is exact on \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1291	
1292	  \emph{Part 2: vanishing.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} the free
1293	  presheaf complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of
1294	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex} is a resolution of
1295	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\) concentrated in degree \(0\); equivalently the
1296	  augmented complex \(\cdots \to K(\mathcal{U})_1 \to K(\mathcal{U})_0 \to
1297	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} \to 0\) is exact in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
1298	  Applying the exact functor \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})\)
1299	  of Part 1 to this exact augmented complex yields an exact complex; by the
1300	  identification \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet,
1301	  \mathcal{I}) = \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I})\) of
1302	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} (with the section {\v C}ech complex
1303	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex}), this is precisely the statement that
1304	  the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{I}\) is exact in positive degrees, with
1305	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}, \mathcal{I}) =
1306	  \mathcal{I}(U)\) supplying degree \(0\). Hence
1307	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and
1308	  \(\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = \mathcal{I}(U)\). This direct route uses
1309	  neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1310	  \(\delta\)-functor identification.
1311	\end{proof}

1315	  \label{lem:ses_cech_h1}

1317	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
1318	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}
1319	  % (read from references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1592--1628).
1320	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a ringed space.
1321	  % Let
1322	  % $$
1323	  % 0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
1324	  % $$
1325	  % be a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1326	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an open subset.
1327	  % If there exists a cofinal system of open coverings $\mathcal{U}$
1328	  % of $U$ such that $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$,
1329	  % then the map $\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)$ is
1330	  % surjective."
1331	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1332	  \texttt{lemma-ses-cech-h1}.}
1333	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and let
1334	  \[
1335	    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H} \to 0
1336	  \]
1337	  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Let \(U \subset X\) be
1338	  open. If there is a cofinal system of open coverings \(\mathcal{U}\) of \(U\) with
1339	  \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\), then the map on sections
1340	  \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is surjective. Equivalently, the short exact
1341	  sequence is exact on sections over such \(U\).
1342	\end{lemma}

1344	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Take an element $s \in \mathcal{H}(U)$. Choose an open
1345	% covering
1346	% $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ such that
1347	% (a) $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ and (b)
1348	% $s|_{U_i}$ is the image of a section $s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)$.
1349	% Since we can certainly find $\mathcal{U}$ such that (b) holds
1350	% it follows from the assumptions of the lemma that we can find
1351	% $\mathcal{U}$ such that (a) and (b) both hold.
1352	% Consider the sections
1353	% $$
1354	% s_{i_0i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
1355	% $$
1356	% Since $s_i$ lifts $s$ we see that $s_{i_0i_1} \in \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0i_1})$.
1357	% By the vanishing of $\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$ we can
1358	% find sections $t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)$ such that
1359	% $$
1360	% s_{i_0i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0i_1}}.
1361	% $$
1362	% Then clearly the sections $s_i - t_i$ satisfy the sheaf condition
1363	% and glue to a section of $\mathcal{G}$ over $U$ which maps to $s$.
1364	% Hence we win."
1365	\begin{proof}
1366	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
1367	  Let \(s \in \mathcal{H}(U)\). Choose an open covering \(\mathcal{U} : U =
1368	  \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) fine enough that (a) \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U},
1369	  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) and (b) each restriction \(s|_{U_i}\) lifts to a section
1370	  \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\); both can be arranged simultaneously because the
1371	  coverings with property (a) are cofinal and (b) holds on any sufficiently fine
1372	  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).
1373	  On the double overlaps form
1374	  \[
1375	    s_{i_0 i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}.
1376	  \]
1377	  Because the \(s_i\) all lift the single section \(s\), the difference
1378	  \(s_{i_0 i_1}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\mathcal{H}\), hence lies in the subsheaf
1379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 i_1})\); the family \((s_{i_0 i_1})\) is a {\v C}ech
1380	  \(1\)-cocycle for \(\mathcal{F}\). By \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
1381	  it is a coboundary: there are \(t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)\) with
1382	  \(s_{i_0 i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}\). Then the
1383	  corrected sections \(s_i - t_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) agree on overlaps, so by the
1384	  sheaf condition they glue to a global section of \(\mathcal{G}\) over \(U\) whose
1385	  image in \(\mathcal{H}\) is \(s\). Hence \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is
1386	  surjective.
1387	\end{proof}

1470	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
1471	  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.}
1472	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, let
1473	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and suppose there is a set
1474	  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of open coverings such that: (1) every \(\mathcal{U} :
1475	  U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) has \(U, U_i \in \mathcal{B}\) and
1476	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\); (2) for each
1477	  \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(U\) occurring in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
1478	  cofinal among all open coverings of \(U\); and (3) for every \(\mathcal{U} \in
1479	  \mathrm{Cov}\) the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
1480	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). Then
1481	  \[
1482	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0 \text{ and any }
1483	      U \in \mathcal{B}.
1484	  \]

1486	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} to the Serre vanishing of
1487	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}: instantiating \(\mathcal{B}\) as the affine
1488	  opens and \(\mathrm{Cov}\) as the standard affine covers, condition (3) is exactly
1489	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. The formal target of this lemma is the
1490	  \emph{vanishing} conclusion \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\) under

1493	  the positive-degree vanishing is needed downstream, and it is all the statement
1494	  establishes.

1544	% for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
1545	% have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
1546	% Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
1547	% By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
1548	% is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$.
1549	% Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module with
1550	% vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
1551	% we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
1552	% another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
1553	% turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth."
1554	\begin{proof}
1555	  \uses{lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
1556	  Say that an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module has \emph{vanishing higher {\v C}ech
1557	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)} if condition (3) holds for it. We argue by a
1558	  dimension shift, taking \(\mathcal{F}\) (with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
1559	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) as the inductive variable and using no input from
1560	  affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing.
1561	
1562	  Embed \(\mathcal{F} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{I}\) into an injective
1563	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\). By
1564	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} the injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has
1565	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every cover, in
1566	  particular for every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). Put \(\mathcal{Q} =
1567	  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving the short exact sequence
1568	  \[
1569	    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
1570	  \]
1571	  For each \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are cofinal among
1572	  coverings of \(U\) (condition (2)) and \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1573	  0\) for them, so Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the sequence exact on sections,
1574	  \[
1575	    0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0,
1576	    \qquad U \in \mathcal{B}.
1577	  \]
1578	  Since by condition (1) every term of a {\v C}ech complex for a cover
1579	  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) is a product of values over elements of
1580	  \(\mathcal{B}\), this section-level exactness yields a short exact sequence of
1581	  {\v C}ech complexes
1582	  \[
1583	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
1584	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
1585	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0,
1586	  \]
1587	  and hence a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology. As
1588	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
1589	  0\) for \(p > 0\), the long exact sequence forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
1590	  \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\): the quotient \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has
1591	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).
1592	
1593	  Now run the sheaf-cohomology long exact sequence of \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to
1594	  \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) over a fixed \(U \in \mathcal{B}\). The
1595	  injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has \(H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(n > 0\). The
1596	  section-level surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) = H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U,
1597	  \mathcal{Q}) = \mathcal{Q}(U)\) established above makes the connecting map into
1598	  \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F})\) zero, whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Applying the
1599	  same conclusion to \(\mathcal{Q}\) (another module with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
1600	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) gives \(H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\), and then the
1601	  long exact sequence with \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = H^2(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) forces
1602	  \(H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Iterating the dimension shift — at each stage
1603	  \(\mathcal{Q}\) plays the role of \(\mathcal{F}\) — gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) =
1604	  0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).
1605	
1606	  The argument never uses affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing
1607	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}); its only {\v C}ech-vanishing input is
1608	  condition (3), which on the affine instantiation is supplied by the standard-cover
1609	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. Thus the dependency
1610	  \(\text{affine Serre vanishing} \to \text{this lemma}\) is one-directional and the
1611	  graph is acyclic.
1612	\end{proof}

1614	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
1615	
1616	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
1617	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
1618	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
1619	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
1620	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
1621	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.
1622	
1623	\begin{lemma}
1624	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
1625	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
1626	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}
1627	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
1628	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1629	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof: exactness of the
1630	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
1631	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
1632	  % \begin{equation}
1633	  % \label{equation-extended}
1634	  % 0 \to
1635	  % M \to
1636	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
1637	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
1638	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
1639	  % \ldots
1640	  % \end{equation}
1641	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
1642	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.
1643	  % It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
1644	  % is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
1645	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}."
1646	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
1647	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
1648	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
1649	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
1650	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
1651	  \[
1652	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
1653	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
1654	    \qquad
1655	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1656	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
1657	  \]
1658	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
1659	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
1660	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
1661	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1662	\end{lemma}
1663	
1664	\begin{proof}
1665	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
1666	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
1667	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
1668	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
1669	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
1670	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
1671	  localisations
1672	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
1673	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
1674	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
1675	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
1676	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
1677	\end{proof}
1678	
1679	\begin{lemma}
1680	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
1681	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
1682	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isSheafify_presheafCohomology}
1683	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
1684	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
1685	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
1686	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
1687	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
1688	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
1689	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1690	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
1691	  % associated to the presheaves
1692	  % $$
1693	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
1694	  % $$
1695	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
1696	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
1697	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
1698	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
1699	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
1700	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
1701	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) of
1702	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has injective resolutions
1703	  (\([\,\mathrm{HasInjectiveResolutions}\;X.\mathrm{Modules}\,]\)), so that the
1704	  derived pushforward \(R^k f_* = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\) is defined (see the
1705	  NOTE on Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}). For every \(k \geq 0\) the
1706	  higher direct image
1707	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
1708	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
1709	  \[
1710	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
1711	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
1712	  \]
1713	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
1714	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
1715	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
1716	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
1717	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
1718	  preimages.
1719	\end{lemma}
1720	
1721	\begin{proof}
1722	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
1723	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
1724	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
1725	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
1726	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
1727	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
1728	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
1729	  = H^k\bigl(\mathcal{I}^\bullet(f^{-1}(V))\bigr) = H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\),
1730	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
1731	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
1732	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
1733	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.
1734	
1735	  The comparison identifying \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\) with the
1736	  sheafification of the cohomology presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V),
1737	  \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\) is constructed directly from injective resolutions
1738	  in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\): cohomology in \(S.\mathrm{Modules}\) is the
1739	  sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level) homology, because the
1740	  sheafification/inclusion adjunction preserves the finite limits and colimits
1741	  computing kernels and cokernels. No presheaf-level \(\delta\)-functor formalism
1742	  is required.
1743	\end{proof}

1745	\begin{lemma}
1746	[Affine open immersions are $(\cdot)_*$-acyclic and degenerate the composition]
1747	  \label{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_openImmersion_comp}
1749	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
1750	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1751	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
1752	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1753	  % L180--199).
1754	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1755	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1756	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
1757	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
1758	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
1759	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
1760	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
1761	  \to S\). Then:
1762	  \begin{enumerate}
1763	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
1764	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
1765	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
1766	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
1767	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
1768	      \(k \geq 0\),
1769	      \[
1770	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
1771	      \]
1772	  \end{enumerate}
1773	\end{lemma}
1774	
1775	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
1776	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
1777	% the sheaf
1778	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
1779	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
1780	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
1781	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
1782	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
1783	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
1784	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
1785	% opens we win." (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L187--198)
1786	\begin{proof}
1787	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
1788	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
1789	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
1790	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
1791	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
1792	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
1793	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
1794	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
1795	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
1796	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
1797	
1798	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
1799	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
1800	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
1801	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
1802	
1803	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
1804	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
1805	  sheafification of
1806	  \[
1807	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
1808	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
1809	  \]
1810	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
1811	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
1812	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
1813	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
1814	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
1815	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
1816	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
1817	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1818	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
1819	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
1820	
1821	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
1822	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
1823	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
1824	  for every \(k\),
1825	  \[
1826	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
1827	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
1828	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
1829	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
1830	  \]
1831	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
1832	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
1833	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
1834	\end{proof}

1836	\begin{lemma}
1837	[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
1838	  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
1839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
1840	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
1841	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1842	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
1843	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1844	  % L180--199).
1845	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.

1848	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
1849	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
1850	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
1851	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
1852	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
1853	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
1854	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
1855	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
1856	  \[
1857	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
1858	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
1859	  \]
1860	\end{lemma}
1861	
1862	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
1863	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
1864	% the sheaf
1865	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
1866	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
1867	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
1868	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
1869	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
1870	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
1871	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
1872	% opens we win."
1873	\begin{proof}
1874	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
1875	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
1876	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
1877	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
1878	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
1879	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
1880	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
1881	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
1882	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
1883	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
1884	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
1885	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
1886	
1887	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
1888	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
1889	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
1890	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
1891	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
1892	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
1893	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
1894	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
1895	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
1896	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
1897	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
1898	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
1899	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
1900	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
1901	  \(k \geq 1\).
1902	\end{proof}
1903	
1904	\begin{lemma}
1905	
1906	\leanok
1907	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
1908	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
1909	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
1910	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1911	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
1912	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
1913	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
1914	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
1915	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
1916	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
1917	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
1918	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
1919	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
1920	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
1921	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
1922	  % $$
1923	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
1924	  % $$
1925	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
1926	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1927	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
1928	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
1929	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
1930	  % we have
1931	  % $$
1932	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
1933	  % $$
1934	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
1935	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
1936	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
1937	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
1938	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
1939	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
1940	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
1941	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
1942	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
1943	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1944	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
1945	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
1946	  \[
1947	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
1948	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
1949	  \]
1950	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
1951	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1952	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
1953	
1954	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
1955	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
1956	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
1957	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
1958	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
1959	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
1960	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
1961	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
1962	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
1963	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
1964	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
1965	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
1966	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
1967	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
1968	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
1969	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
1970	  role in the applications.
1971	\end{lemma}
1972	
1973	\begin{proof}
1974	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1975	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
1976	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
1977	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
1978	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
1979	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
1980	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
1981	  \begin{enumerate}
1982	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
1983	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
1984	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
1985	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1986	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
1987	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
1988	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1989	  \end{enumerate}
1990	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
1991	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
1992	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
1993	  resolution,
1994	  \[
1995	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
1996	      \;\cong\;
1997	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
1998	  \]
1999	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
2000	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
2001	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
2002	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
2003	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
2004	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
2005	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
2006	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
2007	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
2008	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
2009	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
2010	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
2011	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
2012	\end{proof}
